us_producers.notes: Two smelters. $50M investment to restart Mt. Holly to full capacity by June 2026. Hawesville, KY smelter idle since 2022.
Two operational smelters. Part of 4 remaining U.S. primary aluminum smelters.
Only domestic alumina refinery. 1.2M ton/year capacity. Processes imported bauxite.

aluminum.processing_status: 4 operational smelters producing ~680,000 tonnes/yr at ~50% capacity utilization. Single alumina refinery in Louisiana. Down from 23 smelters at peak.
aluminum.recent_changes: Century Aluminum investing $50M to restart idled capacity at Mt. Holly, SC (50,000+ tonnes). Section 232 tariffs doubled to 50% in June 2025. New Madrid, MO smelter shut down Jan 2024.
aluminum.government_support: 25-50% Section 232 tariffs on imports. DPA Title III funding eligibility. Added to all 4 federal critical minerals lists (USGS, DOE, DOD, DOC).
aluminum.key_vulnerability: Electricity costs nearly double competitors ($550/t vs $290/t in Canada). No greenfield smelters in pipeline despite tariff protection.

us_producers.notes: Only prospective U.S. antimony mine. In permitting for 8+ years. Could supply 35% of U.S. demand in first 6 years.
Acquired Last Chance antimony property Feb 2025. Early-stage exploration.

antimony.processing_status: Zero domestic mine production since 2001. Recycling meets only 18% of demand. China produces ~48% of global supply.
antimony.recent_changes: China imposed export controls Sept 2024, military end-use ban Dec 2024. Prices surged 5x from $12K to $60K+/tonne. Civilian suspension through Nov 2026 but military ban remains.
antimony.government_support: DOD invested $60M in Perpetua Resources permitting. $15.5M DPA exploration grant. $1.8B Ex-Im Bank letter of interest for Stibnite.
antimony.key_vulnerability: Zero domestic production, 100% import reliance, China's military end-use ban directly threatens DOD ammunition (200+ types) and defense systems.

us_producers.notes: No domestic production since 1985. 100% import-dependent.

arsenic.processing_status: 100% import-dependent. China supplies 57% of arsenic trioxide and 96% of arsenic metal imports. No domestic refining.
arsenic.recent_changes: Department of War overrode USGS recommendation to remove arsenic from Critical Minerals List due to GaAs semiconductor importance. China export controls on GaAs compounds since Aug 2023.
arsenic.government_support: Retained on 2025 Critical Minerals List via DOD intervention. No direct mining/refining investment.
arsenic.key_vulnerability: Irreplaceable in GaAs semiconductors for military radar (AEGIS, Patriot, AN/FPS-117). China controls both arsenic feedstock and GaAs compound exports.

us_producers.notes: All domestic production in Nevada. Production data withheld to protect proprietary information.
Under development. Highlighted for strategic barite exposure.

barite.processing_status: 75%+ import reliance. India (40%), China (25%), Morocco (17%), Mexico (14%) supply imports. 2.3M tons sold domestically in 2024.
barite.recent_changes: Nevada mine production increased in 2024. American Pacific Mining advancing Palmer VMS project in Alaska. Domestic output still covers <25% of consumption.
barite.government_support: Critical mineral designation enables DPA funding and expedited permitting. No major direct investment announced.
barite.key_vulnerability: Over 90% consumed by oil/gas drilling with no viable substitutes. Gulf of Mexico offshore drilling almost entirely dependent on imported barite.

us_producers.notes: Only significant beryllium mine globally. Controls 60%+ of world production. $65M defense prime investment for capacity expansion.

beryllium.processing_status: U.S. IS the dominant global producer via Materion. Over 60% of global production. Full mine-to-product domestic supply chain. Expanded AlBeCast capacity in Ohio.
beryllium.recent_changes: $65M customer investment from major defense prime for capacity expansion. New beryllium oxide facility commissioned. Defense bookings surged to $75M, backlog doubled YoY.
beryllium.government_support: DPA Title III eligible. Defense prime contractors directly investing in capacity expansion. Strategic stockpile maintained by DLA.
beryllium.key_vulnerability: Single-company dependency — any disruption to Materion operations eliminates Western beryllium supply. China acquiring deposits in Africa (Rwanda, Madagascar).

us_producers.notes: No domestic production of primary refined bismuth since 1997. 89% import-reliant.

bismuth.processing_status: Zero domestic production. China controls ~80% of global supply. 60% of U.S. imports historically from China.
bismuth.recent_changes: China export controls Feb 2025 caused 700% price surge ($3.58/lb to $55/lb). Prices settled ~$17.50/lb after 90-day trade truce. DLA issuing RFPs for stockpiling.
bismuth.government_support: DLA issued RFPs for bismuth strategic stockpiling. Critical mineral designation maintained.
bismuth.key_vulnerability: Leading impacted material in weapons systems by part count. Bismuth solder essential for defense electronics and AI data center construction.

us_producers.notes: Nearly all U.S. supply and ~30% of world refined boron. Single point of failure.
Under development. Initial boric acid production (2,000 t/yr). FID targeted 2026. Ferroboron trials underway.

boron.processing_status: U.S. IS a major producer via Rio Tinto Boron, CA. Single-source domestic dependency. China controls 80% of boron carbide processing for defense.
boron.recent_changes: Newly added to 2025 Critical Minerals List. 5E Advanced Materials targeting FID 2026, launched ferroboron trials for NdFeB magnet supply chain.
boron.government_support: New critical mineral designation unlocks DPA funding, DOE loan guarantees, IRA/IIJA tax credits. 5E pursuing non-dilutive federal capital.
boron.key_vulnerability: Single Rio Tinto operation supplies nearly all U.S. boron. China's 80% monopoly on boron carbide threatens defense armor and neutron shielding.

us_producers.notes: Produces rare earth concentrate containing cerium. Record 45,000 MT REO concentrate in 2024. No separated cerium oxide at commercial scale yet.

cerium.processing_status: Limited domestic separation. China controls 80%+ of production and 85%+ of rare earth polishing powder supply. MP Materials produces concentrate but not separated cerium.
cerium.recent_changes: China April 2025 export controls disrupted rare earth processing ecosystem. Section 232 proclamation Jan 2026. $8.5B U.S.-Australia framework agreement Oct 2025.
cerium.government_support: DOD $400M investment in MP Materials. Section 232 proclamation on processed critical minerals. U.S.-Australia $8.5B framework agreement.
cerium.key_vulnerability: Co-production dynamics mean cerium supply disrupted by export controls on other rare earths. No commercial-scale U.S. cerium separation.

us_producers.notes: 100% import-dependent. No cesium has ever been commercially mined in the U.S.

cesium.processing_status: 100% import-dependent. Chinese company Sinomine controls both active global cesium production facilities (Tanco mine, Canada; Bikita, Zimbabwe).
cesium.recent_changes: Power Metals targeting 2026 cesium production from Case Lake, Ontario with Albemarle offtake agreement (C$5M prepayment). Would break Sinomine monopoly.
cesium.government_support: Retained on 2025 Critical Minerals List via qualitative assessment (insufficient data for quantitative). No direct federal investment.
cesium.key_vulnerability: Single Chinese company (Sinomine) controls global cesium supply. Irreplaceable in atomic clocks underpinning GPS, missile guidance, and telecoms.

us_producers.notes: Zero domestic chromite mining, ferrochromium, or chromium metal production. Only source is recycling from stainless steel/superalloy scrap.

chromium.processing_status: Zero primary production. 77% net import reliance after recycling. Imports from South Africa (32%), Kazakhstan (11%), Canada (6%), Finland (6%).
chromium.recent_changes: South African infrastructure constraints (electricity, rail) limiting ferrochrome output to 60-70% capacity. Chromium consumption reached $900M in 2024 (+6% YoY).
chromium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. National Defense Stockpile holdings. No domestic production investment (zero domestic deposits being developed).
chromium.key_vulnerability: No substitute in stainless steel or superalloys. South Africa (45% of global ferrochrome) faces chronic electricity/rail crises.

us_producers.notes: Only primary U.S. cobalt mine. Shuttered 2023 due to low prices. $15M DPA Title III investment. Targeting Q2 2026 restart at ~1,500 t/yr.

cobalt.processing_status: Zero active domestic production. ~8,000 MT annual consumption with 50%+ in defense superalloys. DRC produces 76% of global supply; China owns 15 of 19 DRC mines.
cobalt.recent_changes: DRC imposed 8-month export ban (Feb-Oct 2025), then quota system (87,000 t/yr for 2026-27). Prices doubled to $53K+/mt. Pentagon seeking $500M cobalt stockpile.
cobalt.government_support: $500M Pentagon cobalt stockpiling initiative over 5 years. $15M DPA Title III for Jervois Idaho. $1B+ DFC commitment for DRC supply chains.
cobalt.key_vulnerability: DRC export quotas at half capacity through 2027. China controls DRC mining + global refining. Projected 10,700 MT structural deficit in 2026.

us_producers.location: Morenci, AZ; Bagdad, AZ; Safford/Lone Star, AZ; Chino, NM
us_producers.notes: Largest U.S. copper producer. Arizona accounts for ~70% of domestic output.
Major copper mine and smelter-refinery complex.
Under development. $35M U.S. govt investment for 10% stake.

copper.processing_status: U.S. IS a significant producer (~1.1M tonnes mine production, 5th globally) but imports 45% of refined copper consumption. 65% of imports from Chile.
copper.recent_changes: 50% Section 232 tariff on semi-finished copper Aug 2025. Phased universal tariffs on refined copper planned (15% in 2027, 30% in 2028). Newly added to Critical Minerals List.
copper.government_support: 50% Section 232 tariffs. $35M govt investment in Alaska copper project. DPA Title III eligible. Critical mineral designation unlocks federal support.
copper.key_vulnerability: 24-year average mine development timeline (slowest globally). 45% import reliance with 65% from Chile. China controls 40-90% of global processing.

us_producers.notes: First U.S. dysprosium oxide production (99.9% purity). ~29 kg pilot scale. Targeting 48 t/yr by Q4 2026.
Heavy rare earth separation facility commissioning mid-2026. 200 t/yr dysprosium + terbium capacity planned.

dysprosium.processing_status: Emerging domestic production at pilot/early commercial scale. China controls 98% of global processing. Energy Fuels and MP Materials targeting 2026 scale-up.
dysprosium.recent_changes: Energy Fuels produced first kg of 99.9% purity dysprosium oxide Aug 2025. Pentagon invested $400M in MP Materials. Prices surged 121% in 2025.
dysprosium.government_support: DOD $400M equity investment in MP Materials (became largest shareholder). $150M loan for Mountain Pass expansion. $8.5B U.S.-Australia framework agreement.
dysprosium.key_vulnerability: China controls 98% of processing. Dec 2025 military end-user ban creates de facto defense embargo. Irreplaceable in heat-resistant magnets for F-35, guided munitions.

us_producers.notes: No domestic commercial-scale erbium separation capacity. Not among near-term priorities for MP Materials or Energy Fuels.

erbium.processing_status: Zero domestic separation capacity. China controls 85-90% of processing. Added to Chinese export controls Oct 2025 (suspended through Nov 2026).
erbium.recent_changes: China expanded export controls to include erbium Oct 2025. Suspended through Nov 2026 but military end-user ban remains. EDFA market growing at 7.16% CAGR.
erbium.government_support: Section 232 proclamation covers processed rare earths. $8.5B U.S.-Australia framework may benefit erbium indirectly.
erbium.key_vulnerability: Irreplaceable in fiber optic amplifiers (EDFAs) that underpin ALL military and civilian long-distance communications. Single point of failure for telecom backbone.

us_producers.notes: No domestic europium separation capacity. Small volumes potentially extractable from MP Materials Mountain Pass bastnaesite (~0.1% Eu content).

europium.processing_status: Zero domestic separation. China controls 85% of processing. Explicitly added to export controls Oct 2025 (suspended through Nov 2026).
europium.recent_changes: China explicitly targeted europium in Oct 2025 export controls, including sputtering targets for displays. Prices $900-$1,200/kg with 10-15% annual growth projected.
europium.government_support: DOD $400M in MP Materials indirectly supports europium infrastructure. Critical mineral designation maintained.
europium.key_vulnerability: Irreplaceable for anti-counterfeiting features in U.S. currency and secure documents. Small market size ($258-270M) discourages dedicated investment.

us_producers.notes: Only permitted U.S. fluorspar mine. $169M DLA contract (ceiling $250M over 5 years). Expected 50,000 t/yr capacity.

fluorspar.processing_status: Historically 100% import-dependent. Ares Strategic Mining establishing first domestic production. Imports from Mexico and China.
fluorspar.recent_changes: DLA awarded $169M contract to Ares Strategic Mining Jan 2026 — first domestic fluorspar supply. Prices up 50% over 5 years. Demand projected to surge 40-70% by 2035.
fluorspar.government_support: $169M DLA contract with $250M IDIQ ceiling over 5 years. Critical mineral designation enables DPA funding.
fluorspar.key_vulnerability: Essential precursor for hydrofluoric acid used in semiconductor etching, battery electrolytes, uranium enrichment, and refrigerants. China (62%) shifting from exporter to consumer.

us_producers.notes: No domestic gadolinium separation capacity. No near-term plans for dedicated separation circuits.

gadolinium.processing_status: Zero domestic separation. China controls 80%+ of refined output. First-tranche Chinese export controls April 2025 (suspended through Nov 2026).
gadolinium.recent_changes: Targeted in China's first tranche of April 2025 rare earth export controls. European buyers facing 20-25% price premiums. Section 232 proclamation Jan 2026.
gadolinium.government_support: National Defense Stockpile FY2024 plan includes potential gadolinium acquisitions. Section 232 proclamation covers processed rare earths.
gadolinium.key_vulnerability: Highest neutron absorption of any element — irreplaceable for submarine reactor shielding (Virginia, Columbia class). Dual-use demand from $1.6B MRI contrast market.

us_producers.notes: Targeting 2026 production from industrial scrap gallium recovery.
Richest gallium deposit in North America. Targeting 2026 production.
GaN foundry. $9.5M DOD funding + TSMC license. Volume GaN chip production targeted late 2026.

gallium.processing_status: Zero domestic primary production. China controls 98% of global primary gallium. 100% import-dependent in 2024. Multiple 2026 production targets.
gallium.recent_changes: China banned gallium exports Dec 2024, suspended Nov 2025 through Nov 2026 (military ban remains). Prices surged 123%. DOE $6M for production R&D.
gallium.government_support: DOE $6M for gallium production R&D. DOD $9.5M for GlobalFoundries GaN. $80M+ total govt GaN investment. DPA Title III eligible.
gallium.key_vulnerability: 98% Chinese production monopoly. 85% of defense supply chains include Chinese supplier. 11,000+ defense parts require gallium. $8B GDP impact from full embargo.

us_producers.notes: Zero domestic primary production. Germanium is a zinc refining byproduct and U.S. lacks zinc smelting capacity.

germanium.processing_status: Zero domestic production. China produces 60% globally. Ranked highest supply disruption risk on 2025 Critical Minerals List. Belgium processes some Chinese-sourced material.
germanium.recent_changes: China imposed total export ban Dec 2024, suspended Nov 2025 (military end-use excluded). Prices surged 300%+ ($1,550 to $2,950/kg). $3.4B GDP loss estimated from ban.
germanium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. DPA Title III eligible. No direct production investment announced.
germanium.key_vulnerability: Highest supply disruption risk of any critical mineral per USGS. 78% of military systems contain germanium components. Irreplaceable in infrared optics for night vision.

us_producers.notes: Active anode material facility. $320M+ DOE support. Tesla offtake for ~70% of Phase 1. Expansion to 45,000 MT pending.
Targeting first integrated U.S. natural graphite producer. Q4 2025 operations target.
Five companies in exploration/development. None at commercial production yet.

graphite.processing_status: 100% import-reliant for natural graphite (52,000 tons consumed by ~100 companies in 2024). China controls 75% of mining and 85-95% of anode processing.
graphite.recent_changes: China widened controls to synthetic graphite Nov 2025, then suspended for U.S. through Nov 2026. Prices at all-time lows threatening Western project viability.
graphite.government_support: DOE $320M+ for Syrah Resources Vidalia plant. DOE multi-billion dollar battery mineral processing programs. $500M for battery mineral processing.
graphite.key_vulnerability: 100% import reliance. Largest component by weight in Li-ion battery anodes. China controls entire supply chain. All-time low prices undermining Western alternatives.

us_producers.notes: Produces nuclear-grade hafnium as byproduct of zirconium processing.

hafnium.processing_status: Limited domestic production as zirconium byproduct. France is the other major Western producer. China expanding hafnium capacity.
hafnium.recent_changes: Growing demand from next-gen nuclear reactors and jet engine superalloys. Supply constrained by zirconium processing volumes.
hafnium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Strategic importance for naval nuclear reactors.
hafnium.key_vulnerability: Production entirely dependent on zirconium processing rate — cannot be independently scaled. Essential for nuclear reactor control rods and superalloys.

us_producers.notes: No domestic holmium separation capacity. Rare earth element produced as byproduct of broader REE processing.

holmium.processing_status: Zero domestic separation. China controls 85-90% of processing. Added to Chinese export controls Oct 2025 (suspended through Nov 2026).
holmium.recent_changes: China expanded export controls to include holmium Oct 2025. Used in strongest permanent magnets and medical lasers.
holmium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Indirectly supported by MP Materials/Energy Fuels rare earth processing investments.
holmium.key_vulnerability: China controls processing. Essential for highest-strength permanent magnets and precision medical/military lasers.

us_producers.notes: No primary indium mine production. Some secondary recovery from zinc refining and recycling.

indium.processing_status: Net importer. China dominates global indium production (~60%). Indium is a byproduct of zinc smelting, which the U.S. largely lacks.
indium.recent_changes: China imposed export controls on indium Feb 2025. DLA issued RFPs for indium strategic stockpiling. Essential for ITO touchscreens and displays.
indium.government_support: DLA issuing RFPs for indium stockpiling. Critical mineral designation.
indium.key_vulnerability: Byproduct of zinc smelting which the U.S. lacks. Essential for indium tin oxide (ITO) in virtually all touchscreens and flat-panel displays.

us_producers.notes: Small iridium quantities from PGM mining. South Africa produces ~80% of global supply.

iridium.processing_status: Minimal domestic production as PGM byproduct. South Africa dominates global supply (~80%). Extremely rare metal.
iridium.recent_changes: Growing demand from hydrogen electrolysis (PEM electrolyzers) and semiconductor applications. Supply constraints persistent.
iridium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. No direct production investment.
iridium.key_vulnerability: Extreme rarity and concentration in South Africa. Essential for spark plugs, crucibles for single-crystal growth, and hydrogen electrolysis.

us_producers.notes: Produces rare earth concentrate containing lanthanum. Separation into individual oxides expanding.

lanthanum.processing_status: Limited domestic separation. China controls 80%+ of processing. MP Materials produces lanthanum-containing concentrate. Used in petroleum refining catalysts.
lanthanum.recent_changes: China export controls on rare earths April 2025 (suspended through Nov 2026). Lanthanum not individually targeted but affected by processing disruptions.
lanthanum.government_support: DOD $400M investment in MP Materials. U.S.-Australia framework agreement.
lanthanum.key_vulnerability: Co-produced with other rare earths from same ores. China dominates processing. Essential for petroleum refining catalysts and hybrid vehicle batteries.

us_producers.notes: Major U.S. lead producer. Last primary lead smelter (Herculaneum) closed 2013. Secondary smelting continues.
Lead recycling from batteries provides majority of domestic supply. U.S. is world's third-largest lead producer.

lead.processing_status: U.S. IS a significant lead producer, primarily from secondary (recycled) sources. Third-largest global producer. Over 70% from battery recycling. Last primary smelter closed 2013.
lead.recent_changes: Growing lead demand from data center backup batteries and 5G infrastructure. Domestic mine production declining but recycling strong.
lead.government_support: Newly added to 2025 Critical Minerals List. Recognition of defense applications in ammunition and radiation shielding.
lead.key_vulnerability: No primary smelter since 2013. Depends on recycled lead. Essential for military ammunition and radiation shielding. Growing demand from data centers.

us_producers.notes: Only active U.S. lithium operation (~5,000 tonnes LCE). Brine extraction. DPA Title III grants received.
Under development. $700M DOE Loan Programs Office loan.
Under construction. $2.26B DOE LPO loan. Largest known lithium deposit in the U.S.
Under development. Historic lithium belt. Targeting spodumene production.

lithium.processing_status: Minimal domestic production and refining. China refines 65%+ of global lithium. Silver Peak is only active mine. Multiple projects in development.
lithium.recent_changes: Multiple new projects in Nevada and North Carolina advancing. Lithium prices crashed ~80% from 2022 peaks, threatening project economics. First new U.S. mines in decades.
lithium.government_support: $3B+ in DOE LPO loans (Thacker Pass $2.26B, Rhyolite Ridge $700M). DPA Title III grants to Albemarle. IRA tax credits for domestic production.
lithium.key_vulnerability: Refining bottleneck — even with domestic mining, processing capacity goes to China (65%+). Price crash threatening project viability.

us_producers.notes: No domestic lutetium separation. Rarest naturally occurring rare earth element.

lutetium.processing_status: Zero domestic separation. China controls 85-90% of processing. Targeted in China's first-tranche April 2025 export controls (suspended through Nov 2026).
lutetium.recent_changes: China imposed export licensing April 2025. Growing demand from PET scan detectors (lutetium oxyorthosilicate crystals) and emerging quantum computing applications.
lutetium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Indirectly supported by broader rare earth processing investments.
lutetium.key_vulnerability: Extreme scarcity. China's export controls directly target lutetium. Essential for PET scan medical imaging and emerging quantum technologies.

us_producers.notes: Only remaining primary magnesium producer in the U.S. Extracts from Great Salt Lake brine.
Israel-based. Some U.S. imports from allied sources.

magnesium.processing_status: Single domestic producer (US Magnesium, Utah). China produces ~85% of global magnesium. U.S. import reliance approximately 50%.
magnesium.recent_changes: China's magnesium production disruptions in 2021 caused global crisis. U.S. Magnesium capacity constrained. Growing demand from lightweight automotive and aerospace alloys.
magnesium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. No major new federal investment announced for expansion.
magnesium.key_vulnerability: Single domestic producer. China controls 85% of global supply. Essential for aluminum alloys, steel desulfurization, and lightweight defense applications.

us_producers.notes: No domestic manganese ore mining. 100% import-dependent for manganese ore. Some electrolytic manganese production from imported ore.

manganese.processing_status: 100% import-dependent for manganese ore. South Africa (29%), Gabon (23%), Australia supply imports. Zero domestic mining. Some processing of imported ore.
manganese.recent_changes: South Africa dominates global supply but faces infrastructure constraints. Growing battery demand (high-manganese cathodes). Added to 2025 Critical Minerals List.
manganese.government_support: Critical mineral designation. National Defense Stockpile holds some manganese. No domestic mining investment.
manganese.key_vulnerability: 100% import dependence for ore. Essential for all steel production (no substitute). Growing battery demand adding pressure. South African logistics constraints.

us_producers.notes: Major met coal producer. Leer South mine is flagship operation.
Leading U.S. met coal producer. Multiple mines in Central Appalachia.
Major met coal producer. Blue Creek mine expansion underway.
Significant met coal production from Pennsylvania operations.

metallurgical-coal.processing_status: U.S. IS a major global producer and net exporter of metallurgical coal. Among top 5 global exporters. Strong domestic supply chain.
metallurgical-coal.recent_changes: Newly added to 2025 Critical Minerals List. U.S. is net exporter but faces declining domestic production amid mine closures. Essential for steelmaking.
metallurgical-coal.government_support: New critical mineral designation. No specific subsidies — industry is commercially viable.
metallurgical-coal.key_vulnerability: Declining domestic production as mines age. Long-term demand uncertain with electric arc furnace steelmaking growth. But no substitute for blast furnace steelmaking.

us_producers.notes: Record 45,000 MT REO concentrate in 2024. NdPr separation and magnet facility in Fort Worth, TX expanding.
Producing separated NdPr oxide. Developing full rare earth separation circuit.
NdFeB magnet facility targeting H1 2026 production.

neodymium.processing_status: Emerging domestic capacity. MP Materials produces NdPr oxide at Mountain Pass. Magnet manufacturing in Fort Worth, TX. China still controls 85-90% of global processing.
neodymium.recent_changes: Pentagon $400M investment in MP Materials. NdPr price floor of $110/kg guaranteed for 10 years. Energy Fuels producing separated NdPr. USA Rare Earth magnet facility.
neodymium.government_support: DOD $400M equity + $150M loan to MP Materials. $110/kg NdPr price floor for 10 years. $8.5B U.S.-Australia framework.
neodymium.key_vulnerability: China controls 85-90% of NdPr processing. Dec 2025 rules ban exports to military end-users. F-35 contains 920+ lbs of rare earth materials.

us_producers.notes: Underground nickel-copper mine. One of few active U.S. nickel mines.
Under development. DOE $115M conditional commitment for battery-grade nickel processing.

nickel.processing_status: Minimal domestic mine production. U.S. imports ~40-50% of nickel consumption. Indonesia and Philippines dominate global supply. China controls significant refining.
nickel.recent_changes: Indonesian nickel oversupply crashed prices. Nickel added to 2025 Critical Minerals List. DOE investing in domestic nickel processing for EV batteries.
nickel.government_support: DOE $115M conditional commitment for Talon Metals processing. Critical mineral designation. DPA Title III eligible.
nickel.key_vulnerability: Indonesia's nickel boom controlled by Chinese investment. Battery-grade nickel processing dominated by China. Essential for EV batteries and stainless steel.

us_producers.notes: No domestic niobium mine production. 100% import-dependent. Brazil produces ~85% of global supply.

niobium.processing_status: 100% import-dependent. Brazil (CBMM) controls ~85% of global production. Canada supplies most U.S. imports. No domestic mining or processing.
niobium.recent_changes: Stable supply from Brazil but extreme concentration risk. Growing demand from HSLA steel and superconductors. NioCorp Elk Creek project in Nebraska under development.
niobium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. NioCorp received DOD $15M+ for Elk Creek niobium project in Nebraska.
niobium.key_vulnerability: Extreme single-source dependency on Brazil/CBMM. Essential for high-strength low-alloy steel in pipelines, bridges, jet engines, and superconductors.

us_producers.notes: Only primary PGM producer in the U.S. Produces palladium, platinum, and other PGMs from Montana operations.

palladium.processing_status: U.S. IS a producer via Sibanye-Stillwater in Montana, but imports majority of consumption. Russia (40%) and South Africa (35%) dominate global supply.
palladium.recent_changes: Russia sanctions disrupted global palladium supply chains. Sibanye-Stillwater facing financial pressures from low PGM prices. Autocatalyst demand shifting.
palladium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Strategic importance for catalytic converters and defense electronics.
palladium.key_vulnerability: Russia produces ~40% of global palladium. U.S. domestic production covers small fraction of consumption. Essential for automotive catalytic converters.

us_producers.notes: Largest U.S. phosphate producer. Major mining and processing operations.
Phosphate mining and processing. Significant U.S. operations.
Major phosphate mining in Idaho. Integrated fertilizer production.

phosphate.processing_status: U.S. IS a major global producer of phosphate rock. Second-largest producer after China. Significant mining in Florida, Idaho, and North Carolina.
phosphate.recent_changes: Florida phosphate reserves declining. Growing demand for phosphate in LFP batteries creating new demand driver. Newly added to 2025 Critical Minerals List.
phosphate.government_support: New critical mineral designation recognizing food security and battery applications. Industry commercially viable without subsidies.
phosphate.key_vulnerability: Florida reserves declining. Growing LFP battery demand competing with agricultural use. China and Morocco control majority of global reserves.

us_producers.notes: Only primary PGM producer in the U.S. Produces platinum, palladium, and other PGMs.

platinum.processing_status: U.S. IS a producer via Montana mines but imports majority of consumption. South Africa produces ~70% of global platinum. Limited domestic refining.
platinum.recent_changes: Growing demand from hydrogen fuel cells (PEM electrolyzers). Sibanye-Stillwater facing financial challenges from low PGM prices.
platinum.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Strategic importance for hydrogen economy and defense catalytic applications.
platinum.key_vulnerability: South Africa produces 70% of global supply with persistent infrastructure challenges (electricity, labor). Essential for hydrogen fuel cells and catalytic converters.

us_producers.notes: Only dedicated potash producer in the U.S. Multiple evaporation operations.
Produces potash from New Mexico operations.

potash.processing_status: U.S. IS a producer but imports ~90% of potash consumption. Canada (Saskatchewan) is primary import source (~80%). Russia and Belarus are major global producers.
potash.recent_changes: Russia/Belarus sanctions disrupted global potash supply. Newly added to 2025 Critical Minerals List recognizing food security importance.
potash.government_support: New critical mineral designation. Food security driver. No major new production investment.
potash.key_vulnerability: ~90% import reliance. Essential for food production (fertilizer). Russia/Belarus sanctions showed supply fragility. Canada dependency is friendly but concentrated.

us_producers.notes: Produces NdPr oxide (neodymium-praseodymium). Expanding separation and magnet manufacturing.
Developing rare earth separation including NdPr.

praseodymium.processing_status: Emerging domestic capacity via MP Materials NdPr production. China controls 85-90% of global processing. Always co-produced with neodymium.
praseodymium.recent_changes: MP Materials producing separated NdPr oxide. Pentagon $400M investment. DOD $110/kg price floor for NdPr. Magnet facility in Fort Worth, TX.
praseodymium.government_support: DOD $400M equity in MP Materials. $110/kg NdPr price floor guaranteed 10 years. DPA Title III funding.
praseodymium.key_vulnerability: Co-produced with neodymium; same China dependency. Essential for NdFeB permanent magnets in defense, EVs, and wind turbines.

us_producers.notes: Largest rare earth mine outside China. Record 45,000 MT REO concentrate in 2024. Expanding to full separation and magnet manufacturing.
Producing separated rare earth oxides including heavy REEs (dysprosium, terbium). Monazite sand processing.
NdFeB permanent magnet manufacturing facility targeting H1 2026 production.
RapidSX rare earth separation technology demonstration plant.

rare-earths.processing_status: U.S. IS a significant rare earth miner (MP Materials) but lacks full processing chain. China controls 60% of mining, 85-90% of processing, 92% of magnet manufacturing.
rare-earths.recent_changes: China imposed export controls on 12 of 17 rare earths in 2025 (suspended through Nov 2026). DOD $400M in MP Materials. $8.5B U.S.-Australia framework. Section 232 proclamation.
rare-earths.government_support: DOD $400M equity in MP Materials. $8.5B U.S.-Australia framework. Section 232 proclamation on processed critical minerals. DPA Title III funding across multiple projects.
rare-earths.key_vulnerability: China controls 85-90% of processing and 92% of magnet manufacturing. Military end-user export ban. F-35 contains 920+ lbs of rare earth materials.

us_producers.notes: Rhenium recovered as byproduct of copper-molybdenum mining. U.S. is a significant global producer.
Rhenium byproduct from copper-molybdenum operations.

rhenium.processing_status: U.S. IS a significant rhenium producer as byproduct of copper/molybdenum mining. One of top global producers alongside Chile.
rhenium.recent_changes: Growing demand from single-crystal superalloy turbine blades for military jet engines. Supply constrained by molybdenum production rates.
rhenium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Defense strategic importance for jet engine superalloys.
rhenium.key_vulnerability: Production entirely dependent on copper/molybdenum mining rates. Essential for single-crystal turbine blades in military and commercial jet engines. No substitute.

us_producers.notes: Only U.S. PGM producer. Rhodium produced as byproduct of platinum/palladium mining.

rhodium.processing_status: Minimal domestic production as PGM byproduct. South Africa produces ~80% of global rhodium. Extremely rare and expensive.
rhodium.recent_changes: Rhodium prices volatile. South African supply constraints persistent. Growing demand from stricter auto emissions standards.
rhodium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. No specific rhodium production investment.
rhodium.key_vulnerability: South Africa produces 80% of global supply. Extremely rare (rarest precious metal). Essential for catalytic converters meeting strictest emission standards.

us_producers.notes: No domestic rubidium production. 100% import-dependent. Rubidium is typically extracted as byproduct of cesium mining.

rubidium.processing_status: 100% import-dependent. Extremely small market. Sinomine (China) controls primary cesium-rubidium production. No domestic processing.
rubidium.recent_changes: Growing demand from atomic clocks and quantum computing research. Same supply concentration issues as cesium.
rubidium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. No direct production investment.
rubidium.key_vulnerability: Same Sinomine monopoly as cesium. Essential for atomic clocks, quantum magnetometers, and vapor cell sensors for military applications.

us_producers.notes: Minor ruthenium byproduct from PGM mining. Very small quantities.

ruthenium.processing_status: Minimal domestic production as PGM byproduct. South Africa produces ~90% of global ruthenium. Extremely rare.
ruthenium.recent_changes: Growing demand from hard disk drives (data storage), semiconductor applications, and hydrogen electrolysis catalysts.
ruthenium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. No specific ruthenium investment.
ruthenium.key_vulnerability: South Africa dominates 90% of global supply. Essential for next-gen data storage (HAMR hard drives), fuel cells, and chemical catalysts.

us_producers.notes: Samarium present in Mountain Pass bastnaesite ore. Separation possible as processing expands.

samarium.processing_status: No dedicated domestic samarium separation. China controls 80%+ of processing. Targeted in first-tranche April 2025 export controls (suspended through Nov 2026).
samarium.recent_changes: China imposed export licensing April 2025. Used in samarium-cobalt magnets for high-temperature military applications. Suspension through Nov 2026.
samarium.government_support: Indirectly supported by MP Materials investment. Critical mineral designation.
samarium.key_vulnerability: Essential for SmCo permanent magnets used in military applications requiring extreme temperature stability (>300C). China controls processing with active export controls.

us_producers.notes: Under development. Planned scandium-niobium-titanium production. DOD investment received.

scandium.processing_status: Zero domestic production. Extremely limited global supply. China and Russia are primary producers. Less than 20 tonnes produced globally per year.
scandium.recent_changes: China imposed export controls on scandium April 2025. NioCorp advancing Elk Creek project. Growing demand for scandium-aluminum alloys in aerospace.
scandium.government_support: DOD investment in NioCorp Elk Creek project. Critical mineral designation.
scandium.key_vulnerability: Extreme scarcity (global production <20 t/yr). China/Russia control supply. Essential for high-strength aluminum alloys in aerospace and solid oxide fuel cells.

us_producers.location: Beverly, OH; Niagara Falls, NY; others
us_producers.notes: Major U.S. silicon metal and ferrosilicon producer. Multiple facilities.
Polysilicon production for semiconductors and solar.
Silicon metal production facility.

silicon.processing_status: U.S. IS a significant silicon producer for ferrosilicon and specialty grades. Import-dependent for polysilicon solar wafers. China dominates solar-grade silicon (80%+).
silicon.recent_changes: Newly added to 2025 Critical Minerals List. China dominates solar-grade polysilicon. Semiconductor-grade silicon supply chain under focus.
silicon.government_support: CHIPS Act funding for semiconductor manufacturing. Critical mineral designation. No specific silicon metal production subsidies.
silicon.key_vulnerability: China controls 80%+ of solar-grade polysilicon. Essential for semiconductors and solar panels. Domestic producers face high electricity costs.

us_producers.location: Greens Creek, AK; Lucky Friday, ID
us_producers.notes: Largest U.S. silver producer. Major operations in Alaska and Idaho.
Significant U.S. silver and gold producer.
Silver produced as byproduct of base metal mining across western states.

silver.processing_status: U.S. IS a significant silver producer. Among top 5-10 global producers. Production from dedicated silver mines and as byproduct of copper, gold, and lead mining.
silver.recent_changes: Newly added to 2025 Critical Minerals List. Silver demand surging for solar panels and electronics. China added silver to export control whitelist regime.
silver.government_support: New critical mineral designation. Silver market traditionally commercially driven. Growing strategic recognition.
silver.key_vulnerability: Growing demand from solar panels (largest industrial use) outpacing supply growth. China/Mexico/Peru dominate global production. Essential for electronics and solar.

us_producers.notes: No active U.S. tantalum mines. Some recycling and processing from imported ores.
Tantalum capacitor manufacturing but from imported tantalum materials.

tantalum.processing_status: No domestic mining. 100% import-dependent for raw tantalum. DRC, Rwanda, and Australia are primary sources. Processing in China and Southeast Asia.
tantalum.recent_changes: Tantalum supply concentrated in Central Africa with conflict mineral concerns. Growing demand from electronics and aerospace superalloys.
tantalum.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Conflict mineral regulations (Dodd-Frank Section 1502). DPA Title III eligible.
tantalum.key_vulnerability: 100% import-dependent. DRC/Rwanda supply with governance risks. Essential for capacitors in virtually all electronics and defense systems.

us_producers.notes: No primary tellurium mines. Some recovery as byproduct of copper refining.
Largest global consumer of tellurium for CdTe solar panels. Vertically integrated recycling program.

tellurium.processing_status: Minimal domestic production as copper refining byproduct. China controls ~60% of global tellurium refining. China imposed export controls Feb 2025.
tellurium.recent_changes: China imposed export controls on tellurium Feb 2025. Critical for CdTe solar panels (First Solar). Prices volatile.
tellurium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. First Solar pursuing supply diversification and recycling.
tellurium.key_vulnerability: China controls 60% of refining and imposed export controls. Essential for CdTe thin-film solar (First Solar, major U.S. manufacturer). Byproduct of copper refining.

us_producers.notes: Commenced terbium oxide production Q4 2025. Targeting 14 t/yr. First U.S. terbium production.
Heavy rare earth separation planned. 200 t/yr dysprosium + terbium capacity by mid-2026.

terbium.processing_status: Emerging domestic production. Energy Fuels began terbium production Q4 2025. China controls 90%+ of processing. Prices surged with dysprosium.
terbium.recent_changes: Energy Fuels producing terbium oxide at White Mesa Mill. MP Materials planning HREE separation. China export controls April 2025 (suspended through Nov 2026).
terbium.government_support: DOD $400M in MP Materials supports terbium capacity. DPA Title III. U.S.-Australia framework agreement.
terbium.key_vulnerability: China controls 90%+ of processing with active export controls. Essential for NdFeB magnet thermal performance alongside dysprosium. Military end-user ban in effect.

us_producers.notes: No domestic thulium separation. Among the rarest rare earth elements.

thulium.processing_status: Zero domestic separation. China controls 85-90% of processing. Added to export controls Oct 2025 (suspended through Nov 2026).
thulium.recent_changes: China expanded export controls to include thulium Oct 2025. Used in portable X-ray devices and laser applications.
thulium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. No direct investment — market too small for dedicated capacity.
thulium.key_vulnerability: Extreme scarcity. China monopoly on processing. Used in portable X-ray sources for military field medicine and industrial inspection.

us_producers.notes: No active U.S. tin mines. Last significant tin mine closed decades ago.
Some tin recycling from electronic waste and solder scrap.

tin.processing_status: Zero domestic mine production. 75%+ import-dependent. China, Indonesia, and Myanmar dominate global supply. Some recycling.
tin.recent_changes: Myanmar military junta disrupted tin production. Indonesia restricting raw ore exports. Growing demand from electronics solder and tinplate.
tin.government_support: Critical mineral designation. No direct production investment.
tin.key_vulnerability: 100% import-dependent for mined tin. Indonesia and Myanmar (unstable) are major suppliers. Essential for solder in all electronics manufacturing.

us_producers.notes: Major titanium mineral sands producer. Trail Ridge operation in Florida.
Titanium dioxide pigment producer with some mineral sands operations.
Major titanium sponge/metal producer for aerospace. Key defense supplier.
Titanium forging and mill products for aerospace and defense.

titanium.processing_status: U.S. IS a producer of titanium mineral concentrates and titanium sponge/metal. Significant domestic aerospace titanium supply chain. Import-dependent for some feedstock.
titanium.recent_changes: Russia (VSMPO-AVISMA) sanctions disrupted global titanium supply. Aerospace industry diversifying away from Russian titanium. Domestic capacity expanding.
titanium.government_support: DPA Title III. Critical mineral designation. Defense industrial base investments in titanium sponge production.
titanium.key_vulnerability: Aerospace titanium market was heavily dependent on Russia (VSMPO-AVISMA). Sponge production capacity constrained globally. Essential for military aircraft and engines.

us_producers.notes: No active U.S. tungsten mines since 2015. Some tungsten recycling from spent cutting tools and armor-piercing ammunition.

tungsten.processing_status: Zero domestic mine production. 50%+ import-dependent. China produces ~80% of global tungsten. Some recycling reduces import reliance.
tungsten.recent_changes: China imposed export controls on tungsten Feb 2025. Prices rising. Growing demand from armor-piercing munitions, cutting tools, and electronics.
tungsten.government_support: Critical mineral designation. National Defense Stockpile holds tungsten. DPA Title III eligible.
tungsten.key_vulnerability: China controls 80% of production and imposed export controls Feb 2025. Essential for armor-piercing ammunition, cutting tools, and defense munitions.

us_producers.location: White Mesa Mill, UT; various mines in UT/AZ/CO
us_producers.notes: Largest U.S. uranium producer. Only operating conventional uranium mill. Restarting mines.
In-situ recovery uranium production in Wyoming.
In-situ recovery uranium production.

uranium.processing_status: U.S. IS a producer but supplies only ~5-10% of reactor fuel needs. 40%+ of enriched uranium from Russia historically. Domestic mining restarting. DOE supporting enrichment.
uranium.recent_changes: HALEU (high-assay LEU) program for advanced reactors. Russia uranium import ban enacted 2024. Uranium prices surged from $30 to $100+/lb. Domestic mines restarting.
uranium.government_support: Russian uranium import ban. DOE $3.4B for domestic uranium enrichment. DPA Title III for HALEU. Critical mineral designation.
uranium.key_vulnerability: Russia controlled 40%+ of U.S. enriched uranium supply. No domestic HALEU enrichment capacity yet. Essential for nuclear power and naval reactors.

us_producers.notes: Produces vanadium as byproduct of uranium processing. Primary U.S. vanadium source.
South Africa-based. U.S. imports vanadium from multiple sources.

vanadium.processing_status: Minimal domestic production. Energy Fuels produces some vanadium as uranium byproduct. China, Russia, and South Africa dominate global supply.
vanadium.recent_changes: DLA issued RFPs for vanadium strategic stockpiling. Growing demand from vanadium redox flow batteries for grid storage.
vanadium.government_support: DLA vanadium stockpiling RFPs. Critical mineral designation. DPA Title III eligible.
vanadium.key_vulnerability: China and Russia dominate production. Essential for high-strength steel and emerging vanadium redox flow batteries for grid-scale energy storage.

us_producers.notes: No domestic ytterbium separation. Heavy rare earth with limited global production.

ytterbium.processing_status: Zero domestic separation. China controls 85-90% of processing. Added to export controls Oct 2025 (suspended through Nov 2026).
ytterbium.recent_changes: China expanded export controls to include ytterbium Oct 2025. Used in fiber lasers and quantum computing research.
ytterbium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Indirectly supported by broader rare earth processing investments.
ytterbium.key_vulnerability: China controls processing with active export controls. Essential for high-power fiber lasers used in defense directed energy weapons and industrial cutting.

us_producers.notes: No domestic yttrium separation capacity at commercial scale.

yttrium.processing_status: Zero domestic separation. China controls 85-90% of processing. Targeted in first-tranche April 2025 export controls (suspended through Nov 2026).
yttrium.recent_changes: China imposed export licensing April 2025. Used in YIG filters for radar/sonar and yttria-stabilized zirconia for thermal barrier coatings.
yttrium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Section 232 proclamation covers processed rare earths.
yttrium.key_vulnerability: China controls processing with April 2025 export controls. Essential for radar/sonar YIG filters and jet engine thermal barrier coatings.

us_producers.notes: One of world's largest zinc mines. Major U.S. zinc producer.
U.S. has multiple zinc mines across several states.

zinc.processing_status: U.S. IS a significant zinc miner but has limited domestic smelting capacity. Most concentrate exported for processing. Zinc is essential feedstock for germanium recovery.
zinc.recent_changes: Newly added to 2025 Critical Minerals List. Lack of domestic smelting prevents germanium and indium byproduct recovery. Growing galvanizing demand.
zinc.government_support: New critical mineral designation. Recognition of role in germanium/indium recovery and galvanized steel for infrastructure.
zinc.key_vulnerability: Limited domestic smelting — concentrates exported for processing. This gap prevents recovery of critical byproducts (germanium, indium). Essential for galvanizing steel.

us_producers.notes: Produces zircon mineral sands from Florida deposits.
Zirconium alloy fuel cladding for nuclear reactors. Key defense/nuclear supplier.
Nuclear-grade zirconium processing. Hafnium byproduct recovery.

zirconium.processing_status: U.S. IS a producer of zircon mineral sands and zirconium alloy products. Domestic nuclear fuel cladding supply chain. Import-dependent for some feedstock.
zirconium.recent_changes: Growing demand from nuclear energy revival (SMRs) and advanced ceramics. Zirconium essential for nuclear fuel cladding with no substitute.
zirconium.government_support: Critical mineral designation. Nuclear energy investments benefit zirconium demand. DPA Title III eligible.
zirconium.key_vulnerability: South Africa and Australia dominate zircon mineral sands. Essential for nuclear fuel cladding with no substitute. Hafnium co-production creates linked supply chains.
